title: The Math Your Brain Gets Wrong About Rejection

questions.question: What is 'Negativity Bias'?

options.text: A habit of complaining too much
The brain's tendency to process negative feedback 3-5x more intensely than positive
A customer who always says no
The statistical probability of failing a startup

questions.explanation: Evolutionarily, our brains prioritize negative signals (threats) over positive ones. In sales, this causes us to overreact to rejection.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT one of the three main 'Rejection Buckets'?

options.text: Wrong Timing
Wrong Fit
Wrong Person
Wrong Personality

questions.explanation: The three buckets are Timing, Fit, and Person. 'Wrong Personality' is usually a misinterpretation of 'Wrong Fit' or simple personal preference.

questions.question: Why is a rejection due to 'Wrong Fit' actually a victory?

options.text: It means you can stop working for the day
It prevents you from acquiring a bad customer who would churn and complain
It proves your pricing is too high
It allows you to pivot your entire company

questions.explanation: Acquiring a strict 'non-fit' customer is worse than no customer. They consume support resources and eventually damage your reputation.

questions.question: As a solo founder, why does rejection feel more personal?

options.text: Because solo founders are less confident
Because the 'No' is distributed across a team
Because there is no team to dilute the feedback, so Product = You
Because customers are meaner to small companies

questions.explanation: With no buffer between the builder and the seller, founders often equate rejection of their work with rejection of their self-worth.

questions.question: What is the only data point that actually reflects a gap in your product?

options.text: Bucket 1: Wrong Timing
Bucket 4: Actual Product/Market Gap
Bucket 2: Wrong Fit
Bucket 3: Wrong Person

questions.explanation: Only when the Timing, Fit, and Person are right, but they STILL say no, does it indicate a problem with your solution/value proposition.
